name: The Franklin Institute, Philadelphia
concept&appearance: Designed by architect John T. Windrim in the Neoclassical (Classical Revival) style, the building was conceived as a monumental square structure surrounding a central rotunda. The exterior features a limestone facade that harmonizes with other civic buildings on the Benjamin Franklin Parkway. The design philosophy centers on the 'Wonderland of Science' concept, emphasizing hands-on learning. A major addition, the Nicholas and Athena Karabots Pavilion (opened 2014), adds a modern 53,000 sq ft wing with a LEED Silver certification and features a kinetic 'Shimmer Wall' facade by artist Ned Kahn that responds to wind.
permanent_exhibitions: 1. The Giant Heart: A 2-story immersive walkthrough exhibit of a human heart (sized for a 220-foot person) that teaches anatomy and health; 2. Your Brain: A 8,500 sq ft exhibit exploring neuroscience, featuring an 18-foot-tall neural climbing structure; 3. Wondrous Space: A two-story, technology-rich exhibit on space exploration, rovers, and the cosmos; 4. The Train Factory: Features the massive 350-ton Baldwin 60000 steam locomotive and explores railroad engineering (recently renovated to expose the floor structure); 5. Electricity: celebrates Franklin’s experiments with interactive circuits and static electricity; 6. Changing Earth: Focuses on climate science, weather patterns, and sustainability; 7. SportsZone: Uses sports to teach physics and body mechanics; 8. Sir Isaac's Loft: An art-science fusion gallery exploring Newtonian physics; 9. Franklin Airshow: dedicated to aviation history, featuring a Wright Brothers Model B Flyer; 10. Amazing Machine: Displays intricate mechanical marvels like Maillardet's Automaton.
central_hall: The Benjamin Franklin National Memorial located in the Rotunda. Designed after the Pantheon in Rome, it measures 82 feet in length, width, and height. The centerpiece is a 20-foot-high, 30-ton white marble statue of Benjamin Franklin sculpted by James Earle Fraser. The hall features a self-supporting domed ceiling weighing 1,600 tons and floors/walls made of rare imported marbles.
special_effects_theaters: 1. Fels Planetarium: Features a 60-foot diameter stainless steel dome (replaced in 2002) with advanced digital projection systems; 2. Tuttleman IMAX Theater: A dome-screen theater (70mm film and digital capabilities) that surrounds the audience; 3. Franklin Theater: A 3D digital cinema for educational films and lectures.
science_popularization_activities: 1. Science After Hours: Themed evening events for adults combining science with social activities; 2. PACTS (Partnerships for Achieving Careers in Technology and Science): A year-round science enrichment and career development program for diverse youth; 3. STEM Scholars: An intensive high school program for students passionate about STEM; 4. GSK Science in the Summer: A free summer science education program for elementary school children; 5. Traveling Science Shows: Live demonstrations performed by museum educators in schools and communities.
